chapters.title: Half of humanity has been left out of the story
chapters.text:
Picture a history book that forgot to mention half the people who built it. That is roughly what standard accounts of evolution have done with women. By fixating on male experience, the field has missed huge pieces of the puzzle about how humans actually developed.

Women's distinctive biology has been steering human history all along. Their bodies shaped survival tactics, social groups, and countless other choices. Without that flexibility, our species would not have made it through the ages.

Here are two quick facts to pull you in. Mammary glands and the ability to feed young with milk go back over 200 million years to a tiny creature called Morganucodon. And the end of fertility in middle age, often called menopause, is not a design flaw. It is a survival feature shared with only a handful of species, including killer whales.

Speak up when the default setup only benefits one group. Push for something better for everyone.

In the chapters ahead, you will look at history through a fresh lens that puts women's biological contributions in the spotlight. You will question tired ideas about gender roles that have stuck around for too long. And at the end, you will find a short guide with tips for learning more about your own body. Let us get started.

chapters.title: Meet the seven Eves who shaped our evolution
chapters.text:
Most stories of evolution lean on conflict and bloodshed, but the story changes when you trace it through the female form.

To find out where a trait came from, evolutionary biologists hunt for the earliest shared ancestor that carried it. Cat Bohannon did exactly that for women. She picked out seven landmark figures, one Eve per major female trait, to show how women's bodies came to be what they are.

As Cat Bohannon has written, we live in two times at once. The present, and the long rivers of deep time behind us.

First up is Morgie, the Eve of mammalian milk. Picture a critter that looks like a weasel crossed with a mouse, sharing the planet with dinosaurs. She is not our direct grandmother, but she stands in for the kind of creature she was. The first true milk-producing ancestor of modern mammals probably looked much like her.

Next is Donna, the Eve of the womb. Donna resembled a weasel-sized squirrel and showed up right as the asteroid hit Earth, though her family tree may stretch back to the Cretaceous period.

Then comes Purgi, the Eve of primate senses. A monkey-weasel-squirrel mash-up whose fossils were dug up in the Montana badlands. She helped shape how we perceive the world.

After that, Ardi, the first known hominin to walk on two legs. She represents a giant leap forward compared to her tree-dwelling cousins.

Habilis follows, the Eve of simple tools and tight social groups. She shared Africa with Homo erectus for around half a million years.

Erectus comes next, the Eve of fancier tools and a more human-like brain. She was craftier and carried a bigger skull.

Last is Sapiens, the Eve of speech, the end of fertility, and rich emotions. She is our closest kin.

As these Eves lived and adapted, they changed the rules for our whole line. They stood upright, handled new habitats, and tweaked their reproductive patterns. Their skulls got bigger over time.

Old bones and stone tools show that bigger brains meant sharper thinking and richer social webs. Despite what you may have heard, studies keep finding that male and female brains look and act a lot alike. Small gaps show up, such as boys lagging in early language skills and girls trailing a little in some spatial tests, but the difference is minor.

There is nothing in biology that says women's brains are weaker than men's.

chapters.title: How breast milk came to be
chapters.text:
It is fair to ask how a body can whip up such a perfect baby food on its own. The answer gets clearer once you dig into the science. Humans have mammary glands because we make milk, and milk keeps infants alive. We make milk because our very early ancestors laid eggs, and because our bodies have shared space with trillions of bacteria for ages. Both of those facts trace back to Morgie, the little mammal-precursor you just met.

Researchers think milk showed up in nature to tackle two urgent jobs, keeping babies hydrated and shielding them from germs. The story of how it all started is full of surprises.

Morgie, like other early mammals, laid eggs. Hers were not hard like a chicken's. They were soft and leathery, similar to the eggs of some reptiles alive today. Those eggs risked drying out before the babies inside could hatch. Morgie had to keep them moist and safe from bacteria and fungus at the same time. Scientists think she managed this by spreading a germ-killing slime over them, the same trick modern sea turtles and platypuses still use.

When her hatchlings broke out, they did not just leave the shell. They licked up the slime as their very first meal. Over many generations, that slime changed. Morgie's descendants started producing a fluid loaded with water, sugars, and fats, giving their young an even bigger survival edge. Eventually, those fluids came from special skin patches covered in fur, which helped guide the liquid into tiny mouths.

A parent is a child's first anchor, so stay close, watch for their cues, and offer a steady, loving space.

To this day, platypus mothers have no nipples. Their babies lap milk straight off sweat-like patches on their skin. That tells us milk did not start out as food. It started as a way to keep eggs from drying out, and over millions of years, it turned into the rich liquid we feed our babies now.

Milk took a turn nobody saw coming, and so did many other parts of you. Save room for a short tip sheet at the end of the book on how to learn more about your own body.

Here is something neat. When a mother nurses, a bit of the baby's saliva travels back into the nipple. That signals the mother's body to tweak the milk. If the baby is fighting off a bug, the milk may load up with extra immune helpers. If the baby is upset, the milk may carry calming hormones.

chapters.title: The science behind the way women store fat
chapters.text:
It is tempting to write women off as just carrying more fat than men, but the difference has a real biological purpose. The fat on a woman's body is not just spare weight. It works almost like a separate organ. The fat that piles up in the hips, thighs, and rear, which scientists call gluteofemoral fat, is loaded with long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids, also called LC-PUFAs. These are the same healthy fats you find in fish oil. Since our bodies cannot make them in any large amount, we need to get them from food. For women, those fats play a key role. They help build baby brains and eyes.

This kind of fat holds tight. That is why it shows up first when weight goes on and lingers longest when weight comes off. But during the last stretch of pregnancy and through the nursing months, the body unlocks those stores. The LC-PUFAs flow to the baby and fuel brain and eye growth.

As Cat Bohannon has put it, women's bodies start prepping for a possible pregnancy during childhood. Not because motherhood is every woman's calling, but because carrying a baby is hard on the body.

In the US, around 190,000 women get liposuction every year. Medical research since 2013 suggests that fat does not return in quite the same way once it has been surgically removed, especially from the thighs and rear. Some women see new fat pop up in different spots, like the underarms. That new fat may not be the same type that was taken out.

That brings up a serious question. If liposuction disrupts a woman's unique fat stores, the kind of fat that plays a real role in pregnancy, what happens if she has a baby later? Despite decades of cosmetic fat removal, no one has properly studied the answer.

The bigger point is not whether women should be allowed to get liposuction. It is how we think about fat in the first place. Should we treat the fat under our skin as something we can just take out without consequence? On a wider level, this should make us rethink what shapes a woman's body in the first place.

Some parts of the body are not there by accident. They earn their place.

chapters.title: Pregnancy is a high-stakes negotiation
chapters.text:
It is easy to assume pregnancy leaves a woman glowing and calm. Many pregnancies do go smoothly, but the process can also be brutal. Some women become seriously ill along the way. One of the clearest examples is a condition called preeclampsia.

Preeclampsia shows up in more than one out of every twenty pregnancies in the US. It sends blood pressure soaring and damages organs, especially the kidneys. Thanks to better research and earlier awareness, most women with preeclampsia now deliver safely, but the condition can still turn deadly.

At the root of preeclampsia is a placenta that is not getting enough blood flow. That often means the baby grows slowly. The placenta senses something is wrong and releases proteins that drive the mother's blood pressure higher, kicking off a dangerous loop. Left untreated, preeclampsia can become eclampsia, which can cause seizures and organ failure.

Pregnancy is a back-and-forth between the uterus and the baby growing inside it. The fetus and placenta push to grab what they need, while the uterus tries to limit how much it gives. If the baby is born strong and well fed, it will probably survive and pass on its genes. But if it drains too much from the mother, it can kill her, which would be a dead end from an evolutionary view. On the flip side, if the mother's defenses are too strong, the pregnancy will not take. The result is a temporary ceasefire, a high-stakes, nine-month balancing act.

Some people think women who never get pregnant are more likely to get sick, but new research pushes back on that. Women who never give birth are actually less likely to develop autoimmune diseases than women who have had children. Studies also hint that women who give birth in their twenties may face a lower risk of certain cancers than women who never get pregnant. One likely reason is that pregnancy temporarily dials down the immune system, which may help the body keep its defenses in check.

None of that makes pregnancy a free pass to better health. It is a heavy physical load with real, long-term effects. Still, for women who choose to have children, evolution has handed the body the tools to handle the job.

If you only look at it through a medical lens, the safest option for a woman's body is to never get pregnant.

chapters.title: Menopause is a feature, not a flaw
chapters.text:
When women hit their forties, their monthly cycles start shifting in a phase doctors call perimenopause. Bleeding can get heavier or show up more often. PMS symptoms can change. New problems, like night sweats and sore joints, can crop up. This phase can drag on for a few years or even a decade.

Menopause comes next, with sharper symptoms as estrogen and progesterone levels swing around. Hot flashes, mood swings, headaches, weight changes, and surprise hair growth in new spots can all show up. The changes can feel overwhelming when you are in the middle of them.

A woman reaches postmenopause once she has gone a full year without a period, and that stage stays with her for life. Today, most women spend about a third of their lives in postmenopause, free from periods, pregnancy, and birth control. For many, it is a relief. Yet from an evolutionary view, menopause is a puzzle.

Most animals keep having babies until they die, since the engine of evolution is to pass on genes. But humans, and orcas, break that pattern. Women stop having babies long before the end of their lives.

One idea, the grandmother hypothesis, says this happened because humans are intensely social. Instead of pushing out more children, older women may have stepped in to help raise their grandchildren, boosting the odds that their family's genes survive. By taking on caregiver roles, grandmothers may have helped shape the entire arc of human evolution, helping their communities thrive.

Every generation is stronger when it listens to the wisdom of the ones before.

A second idea, the empty basket hypothesis, says menopause may simply be the result of running out of eggs. Women are born with every egg follicle they will ever have, and that supply is slowly used up. The natural result is menopause, built into our biology from day one.

Studies on killer whales, another species that goes through menopause, point to yet another angle. Older orcas that have stopped breeding do not spend their days caring for babies, but they help lead their pods, especially when food is scarce or danger is near.

That points to a deeper idea. Maybe menopause evolved so that older women could help their grandchildren and their communities survive. Older women may help their families hold their standing, find food, and most importantly, pass along hard-earned knowledge. Far from being a weakness, grandmothers may be irreplaceable. They remember the lessons of the past and pass them on, giving the next generation the wisdom they need to thrive.

chapters.title: How science left women out of the lab
chapters.text:
In modern biology, the default subject is usually male, whether the work is on mice or on humans. Scientists have a name for this, the male bias. Unless the topic is ovaries or something uniquely female, the female body tends to be left out.

In 1977, the US Food and Drug Administration put out strong guidance against including women who could get pregnant in the first stages of drug testing. The reason was a real-world disaster involving a sedative called thalidomide. Thalidomide was never approved in the US, but it was widely prescribed in Europe and Canada. Many pregnant women who took it gave birth to babies with serious limb defects.

In 1994, the National Institutes of Health, or NIH, updated its rules to require more women in clinical drug trials. Many researchers found ways around those rules, though. By the year 2000, one out of every five drug trials funded by the NIH still had no female participants. And among the trials that did include women, nearly two-thirds never looked at whether the drugs worked differently in men and women. Drugs released before those changes were never tested in women at all.

It can sound reasonable to be cautious. No one wants to put children at risk. But on the other side, this caution has caused real harm when it comes to understanding how drugs work in women. The sharpest example is painkillers.

Many painkillers, including OxyContin, which came out in 1996, were never tested for sex differences in clinical trials because no one required it. OxyContin later became one of the most abused painkillers in the country, often handed to women for things like endometriosis and uterine pain.

A 2012 study found that the number of babies born addicted to opioids had tripled in a decade, in part because mothers had become hooked on drugs like OxyContin. Many of those women had no idea of the risks. They wanted relief, got a prescription, and ended up taking more than men because the drug wore off faster in their bodies.

Studies show that women process many drugs faster than men, but that difference is rarely built into medical guidelines. If OxyContin had been properly tested in women, doctors would have better rules for prescribing it, and fewer babies would have entered the world already dependent on opioids.

Women's bodies are not mysteries. Science just has not been asking the right questions.

chapters.title: A short guide to learning more about your body
chapters.text:
The human body is constantly adapting, surviving, and shifting, and it has been doing so for millions of years so that we could keep going. Here are some ways to dig into that history and learn to love the body you live in.

Hormones drive a lot of how you feel. They shape mood, energy, recovery, and even how you sense pain. Whether you have a menstrual cycle, take hormones, or feel shifts for other reasons, tracking patterns in how you feel can help you figure out what your body actually needs.

Try doing small tasks with your non-dominant hand. Our ancestors had to be flexible, but modern life rarely asks that of us. Brushing your teeth or opening a door with your other hand can sharpen your awareness of how your brain and muscles work together.

Call out bias in healthcare and science. Medical research has a long history of leaving diverse bodies out, which has left real gaps in our knowledge. Pay attention to how your symptoms are handled, ask sharp questions, and push for care that respects evolution and includes everyone.

Look into the link between the body and the mind. Our physical experiences shape our mental state more than we give them credit for. If you are curious about how trauma, stress, and emotions show up in the body, check out the Headway summary of The Body Keeps the Score.

Back research that includes everyone. Evolutionary science is still catching up to the wide range of human experience. Follow and support studies that look at diverse bodies, question tired assumptions, and help build a fuller picture of how we came to be.

Your body, no matter its shape, structure, or history, is working hard to keep you safe and alive. Understanding it is the first step toward appreciating it.
